title: Implement Queue Using Array
description: Implement a queue using an array. Process q operations where 1 x means enqueue x, 2 means dequeue, and 3 means print the front element or -1 if the queue is empty.
outputFormat: For each type 3 query, print the current front or -1
intendedApproachSummary: Use front and rear pointers over an array. Enqueue adds at rear and dequeue removes from front.
wrongApproachHints: Do not remove from index 0 of a dynamic array.
Check empty state before polling.
hints: Track front and rear separately.
A queue removes the oldest element first.

title: Design Circular Queue
description: Design a fixed-size circular queue. Process queries: 1 x enqueue x, 2 dequeue, 3 print front, 4 print rear. Print -1 when the required element does not exist.
outputFormat: For each type 3 or 4 query, print the requested value or -1
edgeCases: Queue wraps around after several dequeues
intendedApproachSummary: Use modulo arithmetic to wrap front and rear indices so freed space can be reused.
wrongApproachHints: Do not keep shifting elements after every dequeue.
You need a size/count to distinguish full and empty.
hints: rear = (rear + 1) % capacity
front = (front + 1) % capacity on dequeue

examples.explanation: After one dequeue, the enqueue wraps the rear around.

title: Reverse First K Elements of Queue
description: Given a queue of integers and an integer k, reverse the first k elements while keeping the remaining elements in the same relative order.
intendedApproachSummary: Move the first k elements into a stack to reverse them, push them back, then rotate the remaining n-k elements.
wrongApproachHints: Just reversing the whole queue is incorrect unless k = n.
The tail of the queue must preserve its original order.
hints: Use a stack only for the first k elements.
Rotate n-k elements after rebuilding the prefix.

examples.explanation: Only the first three elements are reversed.

functionContract.javaSignature: public int[] reverseFirstK(int[] values, int k)
providedTypes: queue values as an integer array

title: Petrol Pump Tour
description: Given petrol and distance arrays for circular petrol pumps, print the first index from which a truck can complete the circle, or -1 if impossible.
intendedApproachSummary: Track total fuel balance and current segment balance while scanning once around the circle.
wrongApproachHints: Trying every start index directly is too slow.
When current balance becomes negative, the next index becomes the next candidate start.
hints: Keep total balance and current balance separately.
Reset current balance and candidate start when balance drops below 0.

examples.explanation: Starting from index 1 completes the full circle.

title: Generate Binary Numbers from 1 to N
description: Given N, print the binary representations of numbers from 1 to N in order using a queue-based generation process.
outputFormat: Print N binary strings separated by spaces
intendedApproachSummary: Start the queue with "1". Pop the current value, print it, then push current + "0" and current + "1".
wrongApproachHints: This exercise is about queue layering, not repeated integer-to-binary conversion.
Push children in 0-then-1 order.
hints: The queue behaves like a binary tree level order.
Each popped string produces two new strings.

examples.explanation: The queue expands breadth-first over binary strings.

title: Rotten Oranges
description: Given a grid with empty cells, fresh oranges, and rotten oranges, print the minimum minutes needed for all fresh oranges to rot, or -1 if impossible.
intendedApproachSummary: Start BFS from all rotten oranges together and spread layer by layer.
wrongApproachHints: This is not a single-source BFS.
Track minutes by levels instead of increasing time per cell individually.
hints: Push all initial rotten cells first.
Count fresh oranges so you know whether all of them were reached.

examples.explanation: The rot spreads outward one layer per minute.

title: First Non-Repeating Character in Stream
description: Given a lowercase character stream, after each character print the first non-repeating character seen so far, or # if none exists.
outputFormat: Print the answer characters as one string
edgeCases: First unique character changes many times
intendedApproachSummary: Use a queue to keep candidate characters in order and a frequency array to remove invalid front candidates.
wrongApproachHints: Scanning the whole prefix after each character is too slow.
The queue front may need repeated cleanup.
hints: Increment frequency as each character arrives.
While queue front has frequency > 1, remove it.

examples.explanation: After the second a, there is no non-repeating character until b arrives.

title: Sliding Window Maximum
description: Given an array and window size k, print the maximum value in every window of size k.
outputFormat: Print the maximum for each window
intendedApproachSummary: Use a deque of indices, removing expired indices from the front and smaller useless indices from the back.
wrongApproachHints: Nested scans per window are too slow.
Store indices so you can evict values that left the window.
hints: Front of the deque always holds the best candidate.
Pop back while the new value is larger.

examples.explanation: Each window keeps only useful maximum candidates.

title: Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least K
description: Given an integer array that may contain negatives and an integer K, print the length of the shortest non-empty subarray whose sum is at least K, or -1 if none exists.
edgeCases: Negative values break normal shrinking-window logic
intendedApproachSummary: Use prefix sums plus a deque of candidate prefix indices kept in increasing prefix-sum order.
wrongApproachHints: A normal positive-only sliding window does not work with negative values.
You need prefix sums to compare far-apart positions safely.
hints: Pop from the front while the current prefix sum reaches the target.
Pop from the back while the new prefix sum is smaller.

examples.explanation: The whole array is the shortest subarray with sum at least 3.

title: Jump Game VI
description: Given an array and an integer k, start at index 0 and move at most k steps each turn. Print the maximum score possible when reaching the last index.
edgeCases: Best previous state falls out of range
intendedApproachSummary: Use DP with a deque storing indices whose dp values are decreasing, so the front always gives the best previous score.
wrongApproachHints: Checking all previous k states per index is too slow.
Remove expired indices before reading the best score.
hints: dp[i] = nums[i] + dp[dq.front()].
Maintain deque in decreasing dp order.

examples.explanation: The best path uses 1 -> -1 -> 4 -> 3 for a total of 7.

title: K Largest Elements
description: Given an array and integer k, print the k largest elements in ascending order of their final heap contents.
outputFormat: Print the k largest elements in ascending order
intendedApproachSummary: Maintain a min-heap of size k. If a new value is larger than the heap top, replace it.
wrongApproachHints: Full sorting works but is not the intended heap-based approach.
Use a min-heap, not a max-heap, when you want to keep the top k largest values.
hints: Keep only k elements in the heap.
Sort the final heap contents before printing if needed.

title: Task Scheduler
description: Given task characters and cooldown n, print the least number of time units needed to finish all tasks if identical tasks must be separated by at least n intervals.
inputFormat: A string tasks of uppercase letters
edgeCases: Only one task type repeats often
intendedApproachSummary: Use a max-heap for available task counts and a queue for cooling tasks that become available later.
wrongApproachHints: Simple sorting is not enough because availability changes over time.
Remember idle time when no task is available.
hints: Each time unit either runs one task or stays idle.
Reinsert cooled tasks when their release time arrives.

examples.explanation: One optimal schedule is A B idle A B idle A B.

title: Dota2 Senate
description: Given a senate string of R and D, simulate the banning process and print the eventual winning party.
inputFormat: A string senate containing R and D
edgeCases: Indices must wrap by adding n
intendedApproachSummary: Store Radiant and Dire senator indices in separate queues. The earlier index bans the other and rejoins with index + n.
wrongApproachHints: Removing characters directly from the string is too slow.
The senators act in cyclic order, so reused indices should be shifted by n.
hints: Compare the front indices of both queues.
The smaller index acts first in the current round.

examples.explanation: The Dire senators survive the cyclic banning process.
